mappings.rationaleAI: Comprehensive coverage from prehistoric and ancient healing practices (A.1) through 19th-century germ theory and bacteriology (A.2), to modern antibiotics, vaccines, cardiovascular surgery, and telemedicine (A.3, A.4).

Established germ theory and identified disease-producing organisms, corresponding to the establishment of bacteriology (A.2.c); developed vaccines for cholera, anthrax, and rabies, corresponding to advances in immunology (A.3.b).
Founder of psychoanalysis whose clinical work reshaped psychiatry corresponds to psychiatry as a hospital residency specialty (B.1.g).
Work of Hippocrates on the nature and treatment of disease and the charter of medical conduct (A.1.d.ii).

The science and practice of diagnosing, treating, and preventing disease covers the entire scope of medicine history (A), hospital specialties (B.1), clinical specialties (B.2), and nonclinical sciences (B.3).
Genesis of the medical school and hospital in the 18th century (A.1.h.i); hospital residency specialties as fields of specialized practice (B.1).
Islamic Golden Age medicine and surgery correspond to reservoirs of medical learning and contributions of Arabian medicine during the Middle Ages (A.1.e.i).
Dentistry as the medical discipline focused on oral health directly corresponds to the history and practice of dentistry (C.1).
Further advances in physiology in the 19th century (A.2.b); medical physiology and pathological physiology as nonclinical specialties (B.3).
Classification of diseases and pathogenesis connect to new doctrines including cellular pathology (A.2.a); 20th-century treatment advances (A.3); public health as a clinical specialty (B.2.d).
The Canon of Medicine as a standard medical text relates to reservoirs of medical learning and contributions of Arabian medicine in the Middle Ages (A.1.e.i).

Vesalius's anatomical revolution corresponds to improvements in anatomical theory and surgery during the Renaissance (A.1.f); discoveries concerning blood circulation and the experimental method in the 17th century (A.1.g).
Antiretroviral therapy and prevention correspond to advances in chemotherapy and treatment of diseases (A.3.a); HIV intersection with immunology (B.2.f).
History of surgery from antisepsis to modern techniques (A.4, A.4.a); emergence of surgical specialties (A.4.b); surgery as a hospital residency specialty (B.1.b).
Improvement in vaccination techniques in the 18th century (A.1.h.iii); improvements in vaccines controlling bacterial diseases (A.3.b.i) and viral diseases (A.3.b.ii).
Medieval monasteries preserving Greco-Roman medical heritage and contributions of Arabian medicine (A.1.e.i); establishment of the first organized medical school at Salerno (A.1.e.ii).
Improvements in anatomical theory and surgery (A.1.f.i); work of Paracelsus and Fracastoro in the Renaissance (A.1.f.iii).
Beginning of systematic medicine in the Greco-Roman world (A.1.d); early philosophical influences (A.1.d.i); work of Hippocrates (A.1.d.ii); spread of Greek teachings to Rome (A.1.d.iii).
The spread of Greek medical teachings to Rome and the acceptance of Galen as medical authority (A.1.d.iii).
18th-century Enlightenment corresponds to medicine in the 18th century including genesis of the medical school and hospital (A.1.h).
Founder of modern nursing who professionalized nursing as a vocation directly corresponds to the history and practice of nursing (C.3).
Pioneered research into radioactivity with applications to cancer treatment through radioisotope therapy (A.3.d.iv); radiology as a hospital specialty (B.1.a).
